notes.anchor: preferring that he, unworthy of that station, should be our friend rather than our slave
notes.note: Quintus rejoices that his brother has freed Tiro: manumission turned the slave into a freedman (libertus) and so a 'friend' who could be a formal member of the household's circle. This letter dates the freeing of Tiro to 53 BC.

notes.anchor: if the loyalty of my Statius is so great a pleasure to me
notes.note: Statius, Quintus Cicero's own much-trusted freedman; Quintus reasons that if Statius gives him such pleasure, the cultivated Tiro will be worth far more to Marcus.

notes.anchor: To Sabinus's slave-boys I have promised everything
notes.note: Quintus, then serving as Caesar's legate in Gaul, undertakes to reward the slave-couriers (probably of a fellow officer Sabinus) who carried the letters; the man's exact identity is uncertain.
